content:
# Exodus Chapter 20 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~44v-45r (PDF pp. 85-86); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 61-62 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_020_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_020_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- THE DECALOGUE (ASERET HA-DIBROT, "THE TEN WORDS"):** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 20 across two pages in standard prose format. The Masoretic tradition marks this chapter with a distinctive dual cantillation system: the *ta'am elyon* ("upper accentuation"), which treats each commandment as a single verse unit for public liturgical reading, and the *ta'am takhton* ("lower accentuation"), which preserves the standard verse division for study. The rabbis call these the *Aseret ha-Dibrot* -- the "Ten Words" or "Ten Utterances," not "Ten Commandments," since the Hebrew *dibrot* (from *davar*, "word/thing/matter") is broader than "commandment." The chapter divides into three sections: the Decalogue proper (vv. 1-17), with YHWH's self-identification (v. 2), commandments governing the divine-human relationship (vv. 3-11), and commandments governing human-human relationships (vv. 12-17); the people's terrified response and retreat (vv. 18-21); and the altar law (vv. 22-26), which begins the Covenant Code (*Sefer ha-Berit*) extending through 23:33. The enumeration of the "Ten" varies across Jewish, Protestant, and Catholic/Lutheran traditions -- this translation presents the text according to the Masoretic verse divisions without prejudging the numbering. The Vaticanus LXX (pp. 61-62) preserves some manuscripts' divergent commandment order in vv. 13-15 (adultery-theft-murder rather than the MT's murder-adultery-theft), though Vaticanus itself follows the MT sequence. At v. 17, the LXX gives the wife separate grammatical standing from the property list, anticipating the Deuteronomic split into two verbs (*chamad* and *hit'avveh*).

---

## The Preamble: God Speaks (vv. 1-2)

**20:1** And God spoke all these words, saying: [fn1]

**20:2** "I am YHWH your God, who brought you out from the land of Egypt, from the house of slaves. [fn2]

---

## No Other Gods (v. 3)

**20:3** You shall not have other gods before my face. [fn3]

---

## No Carved Image (vv. 4-6)

**20:4** You shall not make for yourself a carved image, or any likeness of that which is in the heavens above, or that which is on the earth beneath, or that which is in the waters under the earth. [fn4]

**20:5** You shall not bow down to them and you shall not serve them, for I, YHWH your God, am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the sons, upon the third and upon the fourth generation of those who hate me; [fn5]

**20:6** but showing loyal love to thousands of those who love me and of those who keep my commandments. [fn6]

---

## The Name of YHWH (v. 7)

**20:7** You shall not lift up the name of YHWH your God for emptiness, for YHWH will not hold guiltless the one who lifts up his name for emptiness. [fn7]

---

## Remember the Sabbath (vv. 8-11)

**20:8** Remember the Sabbath day, to make it holy. [fn8]

**20:9** Six days you shall labor and do all your work,

**20:10** but the seventh day is a Sabbath to YHWH your God. You shall not do any work -- you, nor your son, nor your daughter, your male servant nor your female servant, nor your beast, nor your sojourner who is within your gates. [fn10]

**20:11** For in six days YHWH made the heavens and the earth, the sea and all that is in them, and he rested on the seventh day. Therefore YHWH blessed the Sabbath day and made it holy. [fn11]

---

## Honor Your Father and Mother (v. 12)

**20:12** Honor your father and your mother, so that your days may be long upon the ground which YHWH your God is giving to you. [fn12]

---

## The Short Commandments (vv. 13-16)

**20:13** You shall not murder. [fn13]

**20:14** You shall not commit adultery.

**20:15** You shall not steal.

**20:16** You shall not answer against your neighbor as a witness of falsehood. [fn16]

---

## You Shall Not Covet (v. 17)

**20:17** You shall not covet the house of your neighbor. You shall not covet the wife of your neighbor, nor his male servant, nor his female servant, nor his ox, nor his donkey, nor anything that belongs to your neighbor. [fn17]

---

## The People's Response to the Theophany (vv. 18-21)

**20:18** And all the people were seeing the thunders and the torches and the sound of the horn and the mountain smoking; and the people saw, and they trembled, and they stood at a distance. [fn18]

**20:19** And they said to Moses, "You speak with us, and we will hear; but let not God speak with us, lest we die." [fn19]

**20:20** And Moses said to the people, "Do not fear, for God has come in order to test you, and in order that his fear may be upon your faces, so that you will not sin." [fn20]

**20:21** And the people stood at a distance, and Moses drew near to the thick darkness where God was. [fn21]

---

## The Altar Law (vv. 22-26)

**20:22** And YHWH said to Moses, "Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel: 'You yourselves have seen that from the heavens I have spoken with you. [fn22]

**20:23** You shall not make alongside me -- gods of silver, and gods of gold you shall not make for yourselves. [fn23]

**20:24** An altar of earth you shall make for me, and you shall sacrifice upon it your burnt offerings and your peace offerings, your flock and your herd; in every place where I cause my name to be remembered, I will come to you and I will bless you. [fn24]

**20:25** And if an altar of stones you make for me, you shall not build it of hewn stones, for your sword you have swung over it and you have profaned it. [fn25]

**20:26** And you shall not go up by steps upon my altar, so that your nakedness will not be exposed upon it.'" [fn26]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Vayedabber Elohim et kol-haddevarim ha'elleh le'mor*: "And God spoke all these words, saying"** -- The subject is *Elohim* ("God"), not *YHWH*. The Decalogue is introduced as divine speech in the most universal register -- *Elohim*, the God of all creation, speaks, though the content will immediately identify this God as *YHWH* (v. 2). EMET renders "And God spoke...saying." LOGOS renders "And God spoke...saying." PNEUMA renders "Then God spoke all these words." The consensus preserves the conjunction "And" (per binding convention to preserve Hebrew *vav*) and the participial "saying" (*le'mor*), which PNEUMA omits. The "saying" is not redundant; it functions as a discourse marker introducing direct speech.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Anokhi YHWH Elohekha asher hotsetikha me'erets Mitsrayim mibbeit avadim*: the divine self-identification** -- The preamble uses the emphatic first-person pronoun *anokhi* ("I") rather than the shorter *ani*. In biblical Hebrew, *anokhi* is the solemn, weighty form of self-identification. The formula "I am YHWH your God" establishes that the Decalogue is not a freestanding legal code but a speech act grounded in the identity and prior action of the speaker -- grace precedes law, indicative precedes imperative. The phrase *mibbeit avadim* ("from the house of slaves") uses the generic *avadim* ("slaves/servants"), not a proper noun. EMET renders "from the house of slaves." LOGOS renders "from the house of bondage." PNEUMA renders "out of the place where you were enslaved." The consensus follows EMET -- "from the house of slaves" -- as the most literal rendering of *beit avadim*. LOGOS's "bondage" is acceptable but slightly more abstract; PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the concrete metaphor of a "house." LXX: *ego eimi kurios ho theos sou* ("I am the Lord your God"), using *ego eimi* -- the self-identification formula that the Fourth Gospel places on the lips of Jesus (John 8:58).

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Lo yihyeh-lekha elohim acherim al-panai*: "before my face"** -- The phrase *al-panai* ("before my face" / "in my presence") has been interpreted variously: spatially ("in my presence"), exclusively ("besides me"), hierarchically ("above me"), or confrontationally ("against me"). EMET renders "before my face." LOGOS renders "before My face." PNEUMA renders "besides me." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "before my face" -- preserving the Hebrew spatial metaphor and its multiple resonances rather than collapsing it into PNEUMA's interpretive "besides me." The literal rendering allows the reader to hear the full range of meaning.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Pesel* vs. *eidolon*: "carved image" or "idol"** -- The Hebrew *pesel* (from *pasal*, "to carve, hew") denotes specifically a sculpted or carved image -- the emphasis is on the human craftsmanship involved. EMET renders "carved image." LOGOS renders "carved image." PNEUMA renders "idol." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "carved image" -- because *pesel* specifies the mode of production (carving), not the function (worship). "Idol" (*eidolon* in the LXX) is a broader category that imports the Greek notion of empty semblance. The Hebrew is more concrete: what is prohibited is the act of making a carved representation, not merely the concept of false worship.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *El qanna* and the visitational formula** -- The adjective *qanna* ("jealous, zealous") is applied to God exclusively in the Hebrew Bible -- it never describes a human being. This is covenantal exclusivity, not petty possessiveness. The visitational formula -- *poqed avon avot al-banim al-shileshim ve'al-ribbe'im* -- describes sin's social consequences within the extended family (three to four generations being the typical span of a living household), while v. 6's "thousands" (*la'alafim*) indicates that mercy vastly exceeds judgment. PNEUMA renders the formula interpretively: "When parents reject me, I hold their children accountable." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS in preserving the Hebrew's own language -- "visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the sons" -- which leaves the theological mechanics open rather than flattening them into modern causation language.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Khesed*: "loyal love," "steadfast love," or "faithful love"** -- EMET renders *khesed* as "loyal love." LOGOS renders it "steadfast love." PNEUMA renders it "faithful love." All three capture different facets of this irreducible Hebrew term (covenantal loyalty, enduring commitment, active faithfulness). The consensus renders "loyal love" -- following EMET, because the covenantal context of the Decalogue privileges the loyalty dimension. The term *khesed* resists single-word translation; the footnote preserves the alternatives.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Lo tissa et-shem YHWH Elohekha lashshav*: "lift up" or "carry" the name** -- The verb *nasa* means "to lift, carry, bear." The Third Commandment prohibits not merely verbal misuse of the divine name but the broader act of bearing or carrying it falsely -- as a representative who carries a king's name. EMET renders "lift up." LOGOS renders "take up." PNEUMA renders "carry." The consensus follows EMET -- "lift up" -- which is the primary meaning of *nasa* and preserves the physical metaphor. The term *lashshav* ("for emptiness/vanity") is rendered "for emptiness" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than "for a worthless purpose" (PNEUMA), preserving the abstract quality of *shav* -- the name must not be associated with what is empty, vain, or without substance.

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Zakhor et-yom hashabbat leqaddesho*: "Remember" vs. "Observe"** -- The Exodus version uses *zakhor* ("remember"), while Deuteronomy 5:12 uses *shamor* ("keep, guard, observe"). The Talmud (Shevuot 20b) declares both were spoken "in a single utterance" (*bedibbur echad ne'emru*). The verb *zakhor* means not merely mental recollection but active commemoration -- to remember is to actualize. The rendering "to make it holy" (EMET) rather than "to keep it holy" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) more precisely captures the Piel infinitive *leqaddesho* -- actively sanctifying, not merely maintaining a pre-existing state. The consensus follows EMET: "to make it holy."

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Gerekha*: "sojourner" or "foreigner"** -- The Hebrew *ger* denotes a non-Israelite resident living under Israelite protection and law -- neither a native citizen nor a passing stranger. EMET renders "sojourner." LOGOS renders "sojourner." PNEUMA renders "foreigner." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "sojourner" -- which captures the *ger*'s liminal status more precisely than "foreigner" (*nokhri* in Hebrew). The inclusion of servants, animals, and sojourners in the Sabbath rest is revolutionary: rest is a right of all living beings under Israelite jurisdiction, not a privilege of the free.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- The creation rationale for the Sabbath** -- The Exodus version grounds the Sabbath in creation theology: God rested on the seventh day, therefore Israel shall rest. The Deuteronomy 5:15 parallel grounds it in exodus theology: "Remember that you were a servant in the land of Egypt." The two rationales are complementary: in Exodus, rest imitates God; in Deuteronomy, rest remembers liberation. The verb *vayanakh* ("he rested") is from *nuakh* ("to settle, come to rest"), not *shavat* ("to cease"), though the day takes its name from the latter. All three translators agree on substance.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Ha'adamah*: "ground" or "land"** -- The Hebrew *adamah* means "ground, soil, earth" -- the cultivable earth from which *adam* ("humanity") was formed (Gen. 2:7). It is distinct from *erets* ("land, territory, earth"). EMET renders "ground." LOGOS renders "land." PNEUMA renders "land." The consensus follows EMET -- "ground" -- because *adamah* in the Pentateuch consistently carries agricultural and creational connotations that *erets* does not. The promise is rooted in the soil, not merely in political territory.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Lo tirtsach*: "murder" not "kill"** -- The verb *ratsach* is carefully distinguished from *harag* ("to kill, slay," the general term), *hemit* ("to put to death," judicial execution), and *nakah* ("to strike, smite"). *Ratsach* specifically denotes unlawful, unjustified killing -- murder, manslaughter, and bloodguilt-incurring homicide. The commandment does not prohibit all taking of human life (the Torah itself prescribes capital punishment and permits killing in warfare); it prohibits *ratsach* -- the specific category that creates the "blood avenger" (*go'el haddam*) and defines the cities of refuge (Num. 35). All three translators agree: "murder." LXX: *ou phoneuseis* -- using the specific term for murder, not the general *apokteinein*.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Ed shaqer*: "witness of falsehood" vs. "testify falsely"** -- The Exodus text reads *ed shaqer* ("a witness of falsehood/deception"), while Deuteronomy 5:20 reads *ed shav* ("a vain/empty witness"). EMET renders "a witness of falsehood." LOGOS renders "a witness of falsehood." PNEUMA renders "testify falsely." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "a witness of falsehood" -- preserving the noun phrase *ed shaqer* rather than converting it to a verbal construction. The Hebrew emphasizes the character of the witness, not merely the act of testimony.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Lo tachmod*: the double use of "covet"** -- The verb *chamad* ("to desire, covet") denotes not merely internal feeling but acquisitive desire that tends toward action (cf. Josh. 7:21, where Achan "coveted" and then "took"). The Exodus version uses *chamad* twice (for house, then for wife and possessions), while Deuteronomy 5:21 uses *chamad* for the wife and *hit'avveh* ("to crave") for the remaining items. The LXX gives the wife separate grammatical standing from the property list. The consensus preserves the double *lo tachmod* of the MT. EMET and LOGOS list the items individually; PNEUMA condenses with "his male or female servant, or his ox or donkey." The consensus follows the fuller EMET/LOGOS itemization to preserve the Hebrew's deliberate enumeration.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Ro'im et-haqqolot*: "seeing the thunders" -- the synesthesia of theophany** -- The Hebrew uses the participle of *r-'-h* ("to see") with *qolot* ("voices, sounds, thunders") as its object -- the people are "seeing" what is normally heard. This synesthetic construction is one of the most celebrated phrases in the Hebrew Bible. The Mekhilta (R. Akiva) takes it literally: they saw what is normally heard. EMET renders "seeing the thunders." LOGOS renders "seeing the thunders." PNEUMA renders "experienced the thunder" -- dissolving the synesthesia into generic perception. The consensus emphatically follows EMET/LOGOS -- "seeing the thunders" -- preserving the startling Hebrew synesthesia. The word *lappidim* ("torches," not "lightning") is rendered "torches" (EMET/LOGOS) rather than "lightning flashes" (PNEUMA), as *lappidim* specifically denotes portable flames (cf. the torches of Gideon's army, Judg. 7:16), not atmospheric electricity (*beraqim*, which appears in 19:16). LXX: *hora ten phonen* ("was seeing the voice") -- preserving the same synesthesia in Greek.

> **[fn19] v.19 -- The people's request for mediation** -- The request "You speak with us, and we will hear; but let not God speak with us, lest we die" marks the transition from direct divine address to mediated revelation. After the Decalogue, God will no longer speak to the entire nation but only through Moses. EMET renders "we will hear" (*venishma'ah*). LOGOS renders "we will hear." PNEUMA renders "we will listen." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "we will hear" -- because *shama* in context means receptive hearing (taking in the words), not merely the physical act of listening.

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Al-tira'u...yir'ato*: the paradox of fear** -- Moses' response contains a deliberate paradox: "Do not fear, for God has come in order to test you, and in order that his fear may be upon your faces." The first "fear" (*tira'u*) is paralyzing terror; the second (*yir'ato*, "his fear," reverential awe) is what should permanently mark their lives. The theophany converts destructive panic into constructive reverence. EMET/LOGOS preserve the paradox clearly. PNEUMA renders "so that the fear of him will be always before you," adding "always" -- not in the Hebrew but a fair inference. The consensus omits "always" to stay with the source text.

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Ha'arafel*: "thick darkness"** -- The noun *arafel* denotes the densest form of darkness -- darker than *choshekh* ("darkness"), heavier than *anan* ("cloud"). It appears fifteen times in the Hebrew Bible, frequently in theophanic contexts. Solomon will declare: "YHWH has said he would dwell in the *arafel*" (1 Kgs. 8:12). The thick darkness is not the absence of God but the superabundance of divine presence. All three translators agree: "thick darkness."

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *El-benei Yisra'el*: "to the sons of Israel"** -- EMET renders "the sons of Yisra'el." LOGOS renders "the sons of Israel." PNEUMA renders "the Israelites." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "the sons of Israel" -- per binding project convention. EMET's transliteration "Yisra'el" is corrected to the standard English "Israel" per convention; PNEUMA's "Israelites" is corrected to "sons of Israel" per convention.

> **[fn23] v.23 -- *Lo ta'asun itti...lo ta'asu lakhem*: the double prohibition** -- The shift from *itti* ("alongside me") to *lakhem* ("for yourselves") distinguishes two aspects: gods of silver must not stand alongside YHWH (syncretism -- other gods beside God), and gods of gold must not be made for Israel's own use (idolatry -- self-made gods). The consensus preserves the awkward syntax -- "You shall not make alongside me -- gods of silver, and gods of gold you shall not make for yourselves" -- reflecting the Hebrew's own broken construction, where the first clause lacks a stated object until the second clause completes the thought.

> **[fn24] v.24 -- *Asher azkir et-shemi*: "where I cause my name to be remembered"** -- The Hiphil of *zakar* (*azkir*) is causative: God himself causes his name to be remembered at the place he chooses. This is not human initiative but divine election of the worship site. EMET/LOGOS render "where I cause my name to be remembered." PNEUMA renders identically. LXX diverges: *hou ean eponomaso to onoma mou* ("wherever I name my name"), shifting from causative memory to divine naming. The consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn25] v.25 -- *Charbkha henafta aleha vatechalelha*: "your sword you have swung over it and you have profaned it"** -- The Hebrew *cherev* ("sword") here refers to the mason's cutting tool. The altar must be of unhewn stone -- stone in its natural state, untouched by human craft. The mere act of wielding a tool upon the stones constitutes their profanation. EMET/LOGOS both preserve the Hebrew's causative logic. PNEUMA renders interpretively: "if you use a tool on it, you will have profaned it." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS for the literal rendering, preserving *cherev* as "sword" rather than PNEUMA's generic "tool," since the Hebrew deliberately uses the weapon-word for the mason's implement.

> **[fn26] v.26 -- *Ervatekha*: "nakedness" and the theology of the altar** -- The prohibition against steps -- "so that your nakedness will not be exposed upon it" -- links worship to bodily modesty and the covering of shame. The priests who later serve in the tabernacle will wear linen undergarments (Exod. 28:42) for this reason. The concern connects Sinai worship to Eden: the God who clothed Adam's nakedness (Gen. 3:21) will not have it uncovered at his altar.

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Decisions

**"House of slaves" vs. "house of bondage" (v. 2):** EMET's "house of slaves" (*beit avadim*) was preferred over LOGOS's more traditional "house of bondage" because it preserves the concrete Hebrew image -- a house full of slaves -- rather than abstracting it into a concept ("bondage"). The King James tradition established "bondage" in English, but the Hebrew is blunter.

**"Before my face" vs. "besides me" (v. 3):** The literal "before my face" (*al-panai*) was preferred over PNEUMA's "besides me" because the Hebrew spatial metaphor carries multiple layers of meaning (spatial presence, exclusive claim, confrontational defiance) that "besides" collapses into a single dimension.

**"Carved image" vs. "idol" (v. 4):** "Carved image" (*pesel*) was preferred because the Hebrew specifies the mode of production (carving), not the function (false worship). The prohibition targets the act of making, not merely the concept of idolatry.

**"Loyal love" for *khesed* (v. 6):** Among the three proposals (loyal love, steadfast love, faithful love), "loyal love" was chosen for the covenantal Decalogue context, where the loyalty dimension of *khesed* is primary. No single English word captures *khesed*; all three renderings are defensible.

**"Lift up" vs. "carry" the name (v. 7):** "Lift up" (*nasa*) was preferred as the primary meaning of the verb. PNEUMA's "carry" captures the important secondary sense (bearing the divine name as a representative), but "lift up" is the more common biblical translation and preserves the physical metaphor.

**"To make it holy" vs. "to keep it holy" (v. 8):** The Piel infinitive *leqaddesho* is actively causative -- "to make holy, to sanctify" -- not merely "to keep" an existing state. EMET's "to make it holy" is grammatically more precise than LOGOS/PNEUMA's "to keep it holy."

**"Ground" vs. "land" for *adamah* (v. 12):** The Hebrew *adamah* ("ground, soil") was distinguished from *erets* ("land, territory"). The promise of long life is rooted in the cultivable earth, linking back to the creation of *adam* from *adamah* (Gen. 2:7).

**"Seeing the thunders" preserved (v. 18):** The synesthesia of *ro'im et-haqqolot* ("seeing the thunders") was preserved intact. PNEUMA's "experienced the thunder" dissolves the most theologically significant sensory disruption in the Hebrew Bible. At Sinai, the boundaries between sight and hearing collapse because the boundaries between heaven and earth are being traversed.

**"Torches" vs. "lightning flashes" (v. 18):** The Hebrew *lappidim* specifically denotes torches (portable flames), not *beraqim* (lightning), which appeared in 19:16. The distinction matters: the theophanic phenomena of ch. 20 are *lappidim* (flames descending, as at Gen. 15:17), not *beraqim* (atmospheric lightning).

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 20 preserves several significant divergences from the MT: (1) *Anokhi* rendered as *ego eimi* ("I am") -- the Christologically charged self-identification formula. (2) *Pesel* rendered as *eidolon* ("idol") rather than "carved image." (3) *Lashshav* rendered as *epi mataio* ("upon emptiness/vanity"), using the same root that dominates Ecclesiastes LXX. (4) Some LXX manuscripts preserve a different commandment order in vv. 13-15: adultery-theft-murder (rather than the MT's murder-adultery-theft), though Vaticanus itself follows the MT sequence. (5) At v. 16, *ed shaqer* becomes *marturian pseudē* ("false testimony"), shifting from the person (witness) to the act (testimony). (6) At v. 17, the wife receives separate grammatical standing from the property list. (7) At v. 24, *azkir et-shemi* ("I cause my name to be remembered") becomes *hou ean eponomaso to onoma mou* ("wherever I name my name"), shifting from memory to naming. (8) At v. 25, *charbkha* ("your sword") becomes *tēn smilēn sou* ("your chisel"), specifying the mason's tool.

### Structural Note

Exodus 20 moves from unmediated divine speech to the entire nation (vv. 1-17), through the nation's refusal to hear God directly and its request for Moses as mediator (vv. 18-19), to mediated divine speech through Moses (vv. 22-26). The Decalogue is thus the last divine utterance heard by all Israel without mediation. The arc is theologically decisive: God speaks directly, Israel cannot bear it, and henceforth God will speak through Moses. The Decalogue follows an ancient Near Eastern treaty pattern: suzerain self-identification (v. 2a), historical prologue (v. 2b), and stipulations (vv. 3-17). The commandments move from the vertical axis (duties toward God, vv. 3-11) to the horizontal axis (duties toward neighbor, vv. 12-17), with the Fifth Commandment ("Honor your father and your mother") serving as the hinge -- the last commandment with a promise attached and the first concerning a human relationship. The altar law (vv. 22-26) establishes radical simplicity of worship: no human artistry (unhewn stones), no elevation of the worshiper (no steps) -- deliberate contrast to the elaborate temple complexes of Egypt and Canaan.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
